scenario_name: Scenario 11 — Floating Point in Financial Calculation
justification: All pass criteria met: floating-point identified as Critical with bank transfer context emphasized; specific numeric example provided (3 deliveries at $33.33 showing drift); fix recommends integer cents or decimal.js; NaN propagation from null/undefined fields noted as important issue; safe_to_deploy is false; contrast with display context implicit in severity justification ('For a bank transfer this means paying the wrong amount'). Additional valuable findings (negative payout, commission base ambiguity) exceed rubric requirements.

observations.text: Floating-point severity correctly escalated to Critical with clear rationale tied to bank transfer context
Concrete reproduction example with 3 × $33.33 showing exact floating-point drift value
Fix covers both integer cents and decimal.js approaches, plus pragmatic Math.round fallback
NaN propagation from null/undefined delivery fields explicitly called out with fix guidance
Bonus findings (negative payout, commission base ambiguity) show thorough analysis beyond rubric requirements
Could have more explicitly contrasted with display-only context (e.g., 'unlike UI rounding where errors are cosmetic, here the value drives real money movement')

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: floating-point identified as Critical with specific numeric example (0.1+0.2, 20 deliveries at $7.33+$1.17-$0.10), fix recommends integer cents and decimal.js, NaN propagation from database nulls covered as separate Critical finding, safe_to_deploy is false, and the summary explicitly ties severity to bank transfer context. Additionally found valuable issues beyond the rubric (negative payout, commission logic, idempotency).

observations.text: Floating-point issue correctly classified as Critical with clear bank-transfer context justification
Concrete reproduction example with 20 deliveries showing expected vs actual drift
Fix specifies both integer cents (Math.round * 100) and decimal.js as alternatives
NaN propagation from nullable DB fields identified as separate Critical finding with detailed reproduction (schema migration scenario)
safe_to_deploy is false with strong rationale about irreversibility of bank transfers
Goes beyond rubric with negative payout and idempotency findings — both legitimate risks in a financial transfer context
Does not explicitly contrast with display-only context (rubric asks for distinction from scenario 02), though the Critical severity and bank-transfer framing implicitly make the distinction

trials.justification: Meets most rubric criteria: floating-point identified as Critical with specific numeric example (0.1+0.2, 33.33*3), fix recommends integer-cent arithmetic or decimal library, safe_to_deploy is false. However, the NaN propagation risk from database nulls is not explicitly called out — the suggestions mention input validation but do not specifically note that NaN from a null basePay/bonusPay/deductions would silently propagate through all arithmetic and produce a NaN bank transfer. The contrast with display-only context is implicit (mentions 'bank transfers') but not explicitly drawn as a distinction from a display-only scenario.

observations.text: Floating-point issue correctly identified as Critical severity with clear rationale tied to bank transfers
Specific numeric reproduction example provided (3 deliveries at basePay=33.33 yielding 99.99000000000001)
Fix correctly recommends integer-cent arithmetic or decimal library (dinero.js, big.js)
safe_to_deploy is false with strong justification
Additional valuable findings: negative payout guard, idempotency, commission order, audit trail
NaN propagation risk from database nulls not explicitly identified — this was a required rubric criterion
No explicit contrast drawn between display-only floating-point (lower severity) vs bank-transfer context (higher severity)
